claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You do not live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant does not live in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in approved education
  - Claimant contributes £30.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant does not live in the UK; Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant does not live in the UK
Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not in local authority care

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances and your children.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have two children - Alex who is 6 years old, and Blake who is 16 and still in college doing their A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! That's helpful. So you have Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 16, in college doing A-levels).

Before I continue, I need to clarify one thing about Blake:

**When Blake turned 16, did they remain in approved education or training continuously, or was there a gap between leaving secondary school and starting college?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake stayed in college continuously after finishing secondary school - there wasn't any gap.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for clarifying that. Now I need to understand your living situation with both children.

**Do you live with both Alex and Blake in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, no - I don't live in the UK at the moment. I'm currently living abroad, and neither Alex nor Blake live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. I need to understand more about your situation living abroad.

**Are you a Crown servant (such as a member of the UK armed forces, or working for the UK government abroad), or are you living in one of the countries where you can still claim Child Benefit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I'm not a Crown servant or working for the UK government. I'm just living abroad for personal reasons.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Thank you for clarifying that.

Now, since neither child lives with you, I need to understand your financial contributions to their upkeep:

**Are you making regular financial contributions towards Alex's and Blake's upkeep (such as for food, clothes, pocket money, or accommodation)? If so, could you tell me approximately how much you contribute and how frequently?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do contribute to both of them. For Alex, I send about £20 a week to help with things like clothes and bits and pieces. For Blake, I contribute £30 a week to help with their expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details. Let me check the current Child Benefit payment rates to assess your situation properly.

According to the rules, when a child lives with someone else, you can usually get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after they go to live with someone else. After that, it can continue for longer only if you make contributions to your child's upkeep that are at least equal to the Child Benefit payment amount.

**Could you tell me what the current Child Benefit payment rates are? Specifically, how much Child Benefit is paid per week for each child?**

Actually, let me ask a more practical question instead:

**How long ago did Alex and Blake go to live with someone else (away from you)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been quite a while now - more than a couple of months for both of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I have a few more clarifications needed to properly assess your eligibility.

**Who are Alex and Blake currently living with? Are they living with their other parent, a relative, or someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living with their other parent, and Blake is also living with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Now I need to understand the current claim situation:

**Is the other parent (who Alex and Blake are living with) currently claiming Child Benefit for either of them?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, their other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for both of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. That's an important detail. Let me also confirm your immigration status, as this affects eligibility:

**What is your immigration status in the UK? Are you a UK citizen, or do you have a visa, settled status, or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, for example?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm a UK citizen.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit.

Let me explain the situation based on the rules:

**For Alex (age 6):**
- You are living abroad and Alex is living with their other parent
- The other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child
- While you are making contributions (£20 per week), the other parent is already the designated claimant

**For Blake (age 16, in approved education/training):**
- You are living abroad and Blake is living with their other parent
- The other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake
- Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child
- While you are making contributions (£30 per week), the other parent is already the designated claimant

The key rule here is: **Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child.** Since the other parent is already claiming for both children, you cannot receive Child Benefit for either of them. Additionally, you are living abroad, which further affects your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary:**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake at this time. 

The main reasons are:
1. **Only one person can claim per child** - The other parent is already receiving Child Benefit for both children
2. **You are living abroad** - The rules only allow Child Benefit for those living abroad in specific circumstances (certain countries or Crown servants), which don't apply to your situation

If you would like to change this arrangement in the future, you could contact the Child Benefit Office to discuss transferring the claims to you, but this would require agreement with the other parent.

reasonings: Alex is age 6 and would normally qualify for Child Benefit. However, you are living abroad and Alex is living with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child, and since the other parent is the current claimant, you are not eligible. Additionally, the rules state you may only get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or are a Crown servant - neither of which applies to your situation of living abroad for personal reasons.

Blake is age 16 and in approved education (A-levels at college), which would normally qualify them for Child Benefit. However, you are living abroad and Blake is living with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child, and since the other parent is the current claimant, you are not eligible. Additionally, the rules state you may only get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or are a Crown servant - neither of which applies to your situation of living abroad for personal reasons.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for either child. The primary reason is that only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child, and the other parent is already claiming for both Alex and Blake. Additionally, you are living abroad for personal reasons (not as a Crown servant or in an approved country), which further restricts your eligibility. While you are making contributions towards their upkeep, this does not override the fundamental rule that only one person can claim per child, and the other parent has already established the claim. If you wish to change who receives the Child Benefit, you would need to contact the Child Benefit Office and agree with the other parent to transfer the claims to you."

However, you are living abroad and Alex is living with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child, and since the other parent is the current claimant, you are not eligible. Additionally, the rules state you may only get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or are a Crown servant - neither of which applies to your situation of living abroad for personal reasons.

Blake is age 16 and in approved education (A-levels at college), which would normally qualify them for Child Benefit. However, you are living abroad and Blake is living with their other parent, who is already claiming Child Benefit for them. Only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child, and since the other parent is the current claimant, you are not eligible. Additionally, the rules state you may only get Child Benefit if you go to live in certain countries or are a Crown servant - neither of which applies to your situation of living abroad for personal reasons.

response.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for either child. The primary reason is that only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child, and the other parent is already claiming for both Alex and Blake. Additionally, you are living abroad for personal reasons (not as a Crown servant or in an approved country), which further restricts your eligibility. While you are making contributions towards their upkeep, this does not override the fundamental rule that only one person can claim per child, and the other parent has already established the claim. If you wish to change who receives the Child Benefit, you would need to contact the Child Benefit Office and agree with the other parent to transfer the claims to you."
